turns.text: Hello Catarina, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the middle of your pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It is okay. Thank you for asking me. It is strange to talk about this because, most of the time, I just keep it inside my head where the words are... how do you say... more comfortable. 

Being here in Boston, it is very cold. Not just the weather, but the way everything feels so fast. At the doctor's office, they are nice, I think, but they use big words. They tell me my baby is "low risk," and I try to smile because I know that is good, but I do not feel "low risk." I feel like I am holding something so heavy and precious, and I am terrified I will drop it because I don’t understand the instructions. Sometimes the nurse speaks so quickly, and I just nod. I am embarrassed to say, "Wait, what does that word mean?" because I do not want them to think I am not a smart mother.

But then, last week... she moved. It was like a little fish inside, *tap-tap-tap*. It made the apartment feel less empty. I am twenty-two, and I am alone here, but that little feeling, it made me feel like I am actually doing it. I am becoming a mother, even if I am still trying to find the words for the doctors. I just hope they can see that there is a person here, not just a folder with papers.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

In my head, back home, I imagined the doctor’s office to be a place where we sit and talk—not just about numbers, but about the baby. I thought it would be like a long conversation, almost like how my mother would sit with her friends to talk about life. I expected to feel guided, like someone would hold my hand and tell me, "This is what you do, and this is how you take care of her."

But here? It is so… mechanical. You walk into the tall building, you press the button for the elevator, and you sit in a chair that is too hard. The rooms are very white, very bright. There is a lot of paper. They measure my stomach, they listen to the machine go *whoosh-whoosh*, and then they give me a list of things to read. 

The papers have so many words I do not know. "Anatomy," "glucose," "prenatal screenings." I spend hours later in my apartment with my dictionary, trying to understand if I am in trouble or if everything is fine. Nobody ever asks me how I am feeling in my heart, or if I am scared to be alone at night. They only ask about my body. It is a good body, it is strong, but it is not the only thing that is pregnant. I am the one who is pregnant, and sometimes I feel very invisible in that chair.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise... I think it was how much it felt like a business meeting. I expected more... warmth, I guess? When you are twenty-two and it is your first time, you look for a mentor, someone to say, "Don't worry, you are doing a good job." 

But the biggest negative surprise was the speed. They come in, they check the blood pressure, they check the heartbeat, and then they stand by the door with their hand on the handle. It is like they are already gone. I have so many questions, but by the time I can translate them in my brain, they are talking about the next appointment. I feel like a student who missed the first ten minutes of the lesson and now I am too scared to ask the teacher to explain it again.

But a positive? Maybe it was the first time I saw the screen—the ultrasound. When they showed me the picture, the doctor actually stopped for a second. She pointed and said, "Look, that is the heart." For that one minute, the language barrier did not matter. She was looking at my baby, and I was looking at my baby, and we were both smiling. It was the only time I felt like they saw me as a mother, and not just another chart in the stack. I wish it could be like that every time.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I thought there would be a conversation. I imagined a doctor sitting down—not standing by the door—and looking at me, really looking, to explain the news. I thought if there was something to learn, they would explain it in a way that felt like a story, where they start at the beginning and let me ask questions until I understood everything. I wanted to hear that my baby was strong in words I could hold onto.

The reality, though, is much more... cold. It is usually a website. They put the results in an online portal, and suddenly there are all these numbers and red exclamation marks. I see words like "normal" or "pending," and I panic. I do not know if "normal" means it is perfectly fine or if it is just okay. Then, the next time I see the nurse, she just says, "Everything looks good, keep doing what you are doing," and she moves on to the next task. 

I never know if she is just saying that to be fast, or if she truly looked at the results for me. It feels like I am navigating a maze in the dark. I have to be my own doctor, my own translator, and my own comfort, all at once. It is very heavy.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

Yes, a very big gap. It is like they give me a heavy book in a language I do not know and say, "Read this, it is important." 

They give me many papers—so many papers—about what to eat, what not to eat, what to watch for, but they are all written in very medical, very fast English. I feel like the information is *there*, but it is trapped behind a wall. I need someone to talk to me, not just give me a printout to take home. When I get home to my empty apartment, the papers just sit on the table. If I have a question—like, if my belly feels tight or if I am worried about a pain—I do not know who to ask. Do I call the office? Will they think I am just a nervous girl? 

I want to know *why* things are happening. Why do they test the sugar? Why do they look at the scan this way? I feel like I am just following orders instead of learning how to grow this baby. I have enough information to be scared, but I do not have enough information to feel calm. I think if they just sat with me for five minutes—not ten seconds—and let me ask my "stupid" questions, I would feel much, much safer.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I thought that since I am carrying a person, I would be assigned to a person, too. I expected a doctor or a midwife—somebody who would learn my face. I thought they would remember, "Oh, this is Catarina, she is alone here, she is doing this by herself." I wanted someone who would ask, "How are you sleeping?" or "Do you have enough food in the house?" 

But here, it feels like I am just a guest at a desk. Every time I go, it is a different face. One day it is a doctor, the next day it is a nurse practitioner, the next day someone I have never seen before. I have to start the story from the beginning every time. "Yes, this is my first baby. Yes, I am22. Yes, I live in Boston." It is exhausting to keep introducing myself to people who should know me. 

There is no continuity. It is like passing a ball in a game, but sometimes the ball drops. Nobody knows that I was scared last week, or that I finally felt the baby move. I am just a file folder that gets picked up by whoever is standing there. It makes me feel like my care is not a relationship, it is just a task they have to finish before they go home. I don't have a "care provider"—I have a rotating door.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It makes me feel smaller, like I am just a project to be completed rather than a person becoming a mother. When you see a different face every time, you stop trying to build a bridge. You stop being honest. If I am worried about a little pain, or if I feel sad or overwhelmed in my apartment at night, I don't tell them. Why should I? They don't know me. They don't know that I’m trying so hard to learn this language just to keep my baby safe.

Practically, it is a mess. I feel like I am repeating the same things, and sometimes, they tell me different things! One person says one thing, and the next person says, "Oh, don't worry about that." It makes me lose my trust in the whole place. I start to wonder if they even read the notes from the last person. 

Emotionally, it makes the loneliness worse. I came here expecting that I would be taken under someone’s wing, like how my mother was looked after. Instead, I feel like a stranger asking for directions in a place where everyone is too busy to stop. I walk out of that hospital and I feel like I am back in the cold, carrying this secret, growing weight all by myself. It is very hard to be brave when you have no one in your corner.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected, maybe, for there to be some acknowledgment that this is a miracle—or at least a change. Back home, my mother told me how the doctor would press on the belly and talk to the baby, or comment on how the shape of the stomach tells a story. I thought they would talk to me about my body like it was a teammate, something we were working on together.

But in these appointments, the exam feels… clinical. Very cold. They measure the height of the belly with a tape, they mark it on a graph, and they focus on the numbers. It’s like they are inspecting a machine to see if the parts are moving correctly. They don't look at *me*; they look at the tape measure. 

Sometimes, I feel very exposed, just a body to be poked and prodded. They touch my stomach without really saying, "I am going to touch you now," and it makes me feel disconnected from my own skin. It’s not that they are mean, they are just… indifferent. When I look at my changing body in the mirror at home, I see my daughter growing. When I am on the table at the hospital, I feel like I am just an object that they are measuring to see if I fit the "low risk" criteria. It is a lonely way to grow a life.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

There was one time… I think it was around the twentieth week. They did an internal exam, and I did not know it was going to happen. I thought it was just going to be the same quick check with the tape measure. The doctor—I had never seen her before—just told me to move down, and then she did what she had to do. It was all very fast, and I was so embarrassed I couldn't even look at her face. 

She did not ask me if I was ready, or explain why, or even talk to me while she was doing it. I felt very small. I am a young woman, and I have never been through this, so everything feels very personal. When they handle me like I am just a thing in a room, it makes me feel like I have lost control of my own body. 

I wanted to say, "Please, tell me what you are doing," but my English—the words just stuck in my throat. I just stared at the ceiling tiles and counted them until it was over. I went home and cried, not because it hurt, but because it felt so impersonal. It made me feel like I was not a participant in my own pregnancy, just a place for them to do their work. That is not how I want to feel when I am growing my little girl.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I have not been told I have those things yet—they always tell me "low risk"—but that is actually part of what makes me scared. I read things online, or I hear other women talking, and I worry. Because I am twenty-two and I don't always understand the doctors, I am terrified that I might have something and just not know the word for it. 

I imagine that if I *did* have something like that, the experience would be even more isolating. If I already feel like I am fighting to be heard when everything is "normal," what would happen if things became complicated? I think I would just be buried in even more papers, even more instructions, and even more, how do you say... panic? 

I think the biggest difference between what I expect and reality is that I expect care to be a bridge, but right now it feels like a wall. If I had a condition, I would need someone to sit with me, maybe someone who speaks my language, to explain, "This is what you eat, this is how you take care of your blood." Without that, I feel like I would be walking in a fog. Even with a "low risk" pregnancy, I feel like I am just hoping I don't miss a sign, because there is no one there to hold my hand and guide me through the dark.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I thought there would be a number I could call, a direct line to someone who knew my name, where a friendly voice would tell me, "Don't worry, come in, let's look." I imagined that because I am a first-time mother in a new country, they would understand that I am always a little bit nervous. I thought help would be easy to find.

The reality is... difficult. When I call, I reach a machine, or a person who does not know who I am. I have to explain, "My name is Catarina, I am pregnant," and then I get put on hold for a long time. The music is very loud and makes me feel more anxious. When I finally talk to someone, they ask, "Is it an emergency?" and I never know what to say. If it is not blood, is it an emergency? I am afraid to say "yes" and look like a hysterical girl, but I am afraid to say "no" and then regret not being seen.

Last week, when I had a sharp pain in my side, I waited four hours for a return call. I sat in my room, staring at my phone, trying to decide if I should go to the hospital alone. It made me feel so disconnected. The system is not built for someone like me—someone who needs a little extra time and a lot of reassurance. It is built for people who know exactly what is normal and what is not, and I am still just learning. It feels like the door is locked when I need it most.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

Back home, I always imagined that being pregnant would be a celebration. I pictured my mother, my aunts, and my friends coming over to my house, touching my belly, helping me prepare for the baby, and bringing me food. I thought there would be so much laughter and shared wisdom. I thought I would never be alone.

But here in Boston, the reality is very quiet. My family is far away, across the ocean. We talk on the phone, but a screen is not the same as a warm hand on my shoulder. They worry about me, and that makes me worry about them, so I often lie and say everything is fine, even when I am tired or scared. I don’t want them to feel helpless from so far away.

My friends here are mostly students or busy with work, and they don't know anything about being a mother. They are kind, but they do not *understand*. They cannot tell me how to handle the swelling, or what that "quickening" feeling really means. There is no community of mothers around me, just the cold streets and my empty apartment. Sometimes, I sit in the park and watch other women with their babies or their mothers, and I feel a little bit like a ghost. I am building a life for my daughter, but I am doing it in total silence. It is the hardest part—not having anyone here to say, "I am proud of you."
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I did not expect that I would have to be the one to give the comfort. When I tell my mother about the baby, she gets so excited, and then she starts crying because she is not here to help me. So, I have to be the one to tell *her* that I am fine, that I am eating well, that the doctors are good. I am twenty-two, and I am the one being the strong one for my own mother. That was not the plan.

I also did not expect the kindness of a stranger, actually. A lady at the laundry mat, she saw my stomach and she just... smiled. She did not know my name, she did not know I was scared, but she gave me a look that said, "You are doing okay." It was the only time I felt truly seen by someone without a clipboard in their hand. 

But the support I expected—the real, day-to-day help—did not come. I thought my friends would be over all the time, asking questions and laughing, but they are so busy with their own lives. They do not know how to be around a pregnant woman. They look at my stomach like it is something delicate and scary. Sometimes, I just want to sit on a couch with someone who will make me tea and not talk about medical charts. I miss having someone to ask, "Is this pain normal?" without having to worry if I am annoying them. It turns out, motherhood is much quieter than I imagined.

Sometimes I wonder if the baby can feel it—the silence in the room, I mean. When I am sitting on the edge of my bed, staring at the little pile of clothes I bought, I wonder if she knows her mother is just guessing at everything. 

It is strange, because when I talk to her—when I speak my own language to her—I feel like a real person again. I tell her about my mother’s garden back home, about the way the light hits the trees in the morning, and the "tap-tap" comes back. It is like she is the only one who really knows me here. The doctors, they know my blood pressure, they know my weight, but they don't know that I am trying to keep her safe in a city that feels like it’s trying to freeze me out.

I worry, too, about when the time comes—when I go into the hospital. I have these nightmares that I won’t be able to say "I am scared" or "I need help" because the English words will just disappear. I am trying to read, to learn, but it is hard when you are so tired. I just hope that when I finally walk through those doors for the last time, the people there will look at me—really look at me—and see that I am a mother, and that I am doing my absolute best. I want someone to hold my hand, just once, and tell me that everything is going to be okay. Not because a chart says so, but because they believe it, too.
Were there moments when a doctor, partner, or family member's opinion overrode yours — even when you hadn't asked for their input?

It happens more than I like to admit. Because I am young, and because my English is not the fastest, people seem to assume I do not have a mind of my own. 

There was one time at the clinic, I told the doctor I was feeling very tired—the kind of tired that feels like heavy stones in your legs. I just wanted to ask if it was okay, if there was something I could eat to help, or if I should rest more. Before I could even finish my sentence, the doctor looked at her screen, typed something, and said, "It is normal. You are working, you are pregnant, just take some iron pills." 

She didn't ask me *how* I was working, or if I had any help, or if the stress was making my heart race. She just gave me a pill and a dismissal. I felt like I was being treated like a machine that needed a little oil, not a woman who was struggling to balance everything. It felt like she decided what I needed before I even finished explaining how it felt to be me. 

And my friends... sometimes they say things like, "Oh, you should be doing this," or "Why are you eating that?" and they say it with such confidence, like they know my body better than I do. It makes me feel like I am failing. I have to bite my tongue and just say "okay," because it is easier than fighting to explain why I do things the way I do. It is like everyone has an opinion on my pregnancy, but nobody is actually listening to the mother inside.

I think the hardest part is that I am starting to feel like I am losing my own voice. In my own language, I am strong. I can tell people what I need, I can explain how I feel, and I know exactly what I am looking for. But here, I am learning to be quiet. I am learning to just nod and say "Okay" because that is the path of least resistance. 

Sometimes I worry that when I am finally in labor, that will be the moment I need my voice the most—and I will have forgotten how to use it. I fear that they will make decisions for me while I am in pain, and I will just let it happen because I am too tired to translate the situation in my head. 

I keep wondering: what does a "good" patient look like to them? Is it one who doesn't ask questions? Is it one who doesn't show fear? Because if it is, then I am failing. I have so many questions that I don't ask because I don't want to seem slow or difficult. And yet, I am carrying this life, this beautiful little heart, and I am the only one who knows the rhythm of her movements. 

I don't want to be a "good patient." I want to be a mother. I want to look into the eyes of the person helping me bring her into the world, and I want them to know that I am not just a set of statistics or a barrier to their schedule. I am a woman, I am twenty-two, and I am learning to love someone more than I ever knew possible. Why is that so hard for them to see?
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

I don’t have a partner here with me. That is part of the silence in my apartment, I think. When I talk to people—even the doctors sometimes—they look for a name on the chart, a husband or a father, and when they see there is no one, I feel their eyebrows go up, just a little. They don’t say anything, but the air in the room changes. It’s like they think I made a mistake or that I am even more lost because I am doing this alone.

Because I don't have a partner, the "pressure" is different. It comes from the expectations of the doctors and the system. They talk as if there is a "normal" way to do this, and because I don’t fit that picture, I feel like I am constantly apologizing for existing. 

I’ve had to make all these choices on my own—about my work, about where I live, about how I manage my health. There is no one to talk to about the hard stuff, no one to tell me, "You are doing fine." So, I just carry it. I feel the pressure to be a perfect, quiet, obedient patient because I am scared that if I am "difficult," they will treat me even worse or judge me for being a young, single mother. I have to be perfect, or else they might see me as a problem. It’s a very heavy way to carry a baby.

Sometimes I sit here, just staring at my hands, and I think about how much I have changed in these last few months. Before, I was just a girl living in a new city, trying to find my way. Now, my body is doing something that feels ancient and important, but the people who are supposed to help me are only focused on the "now"—the blood pressure, the weight, the next appointment.

I want to tell them about the dream I had last night—the one where I am back home, and my mother is sitting next to me, just rubbing my back while I talk. But here? If I tried to say that to the nurse, she would probably just mark "Anxiety: High" on her screen. And then I would be labeled, and the label would stay, and I would be even more of a "case" to be managed.

So I keep my mouth shut. I focus on being the "easy" patient. I show up on time, I nod when they talk, I pay my bills, and I go home. It feels like I am performing a play, but I don’t know all the lines. I am terrified that when the baby comes—when it is finally time to be her mother, to protect her, to speak for her—that I will still be trapped in this performance. 

But then, I feel that little *tap-tap* again, that little heartbeat, and I remember. She doesn't need me to be a "good patient" for them. She needs me to be *her* mother. And maybe that is enough. Maybe I don't need them to understand me; I just need to make sure that I don't forget who I am. I am Catarina. I am twenty-two. And I am going to be the best mother I can, even if I have to do it in a language that doesn't quite fit.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

They always talk about my weight. It feels like the first thing they look at when I walk in. They look at the chart, they look at the scale, and then they say something like, "You are gaining a little too much," or "We need to be careful with your diet." 

It lands... like a cold, heavy stone in my stomach. I am twenty-two, and I am already nervous about everything, and then they make me feel like I am failing at the one thing I should be doing "right." They don't ask what I am eating. They don't ask if I am tired from working and only have time for simple things, or if I am craving things that make me feel close to home. They just give me a list of "no's" and "should nots."

It makes me want to hide. When they comment on my body, it feels like they are critiquing my daughter’s home. I want to tell them, "I am growing a person! I am doing the hardest work of my life!" but instead, I just look at my shoes. I feel like they see a number, not a mother. Every time they mention my weight, I feel a little smaller, a little more ashamed. It's like they think if they don't control me, I'll mess it all up. I already feel like I’m walking on glass; I don't need them to make me feel like I’m also too big, or too small, or just... not good enough.

I think that is why I am so quiet now. When you spend so much time being corrected—about your weight, about the way you speak, about how you should be "managing"—you stop wanting to share anything at all. You start to feel like you are just a collection of mistakes that they have to fix before the baby comes. 

Sometimes I wonder, if I were older, or if I had a husband sitting next to me, or if my English were better... would they talk to me with more respect? Would they ask how I *feel* instead of telling me what to fix? It makes me angry, sometimes. I am twenty-two, I am doing all of this by myself in a city that is not my home, and I am working every day to make sure my daughter has a good life. That is not a mistake. That is strength.

I don’t know. I feel like I am in a tunnel, and I am just waiting to see the light at the other end. I just want to be a mother. I want to look at her and know that I didn't let them make me feel small. I want to be able to tell her stories about the garden at home, and I want her to know that her mother, even when she was scared and alone, was brave enough to keep going. I just hope that by then, I find my voice again.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I think the first person I turn to is always my mother, even though she is thousands of miles away and doesn't know the specifics of this hospital. When I have a question—*is this normal?*—I call her. She doesn’t know the English words or the American "way," but she knows *motherhood*. She knows how to tell me to breathe, how to tell me that my body is doing exactly what it needs to do. She is my only bridge to feeling like myself, rather than just a patient.

Beyond her, I go to the internet. But not the official doctor websites—they are too cold, too full of red warning signs. I look for videos, for women who talk like me, or who are in the same situation. I find comfort in forums where other young women share their stories about feeling invisible or scared in the clinic. It helps to know that I am not the only one feeling this way. 

Sometimes, I turn to a small notebook I keep in my bag. I write down the words I didn't understand at the clinic so I can look them up slowly, without the pressure of a nurse waiting by the door. It is my private rebellion, I think. I am building my own dictionary of this journey, one where I am the one in control of the information, not them. It feels like I am taking back a little piece of my own story, away from the charts and the "low risk" labels.

I think I’m realizing that my real "healthcare" isn't happening in that hospital at all. It’s happening in my kitchen at 2:00 AM, when I’m drinking herbal tea and reading about how my daughter’s lungs are developing, or when I’m whispering to my stomach about the life we’re going to have together. That’s where the care is. That’s where the warmth is.

If I had to tell the hospital one thing—if I could truly use my voice without being afraid—I would tell them to stop treating me like a broken part that needs to be fixed. I am not a broken part. I am a bridge. I am the place where a new life is starting, and I wish they would look at me with curiosity instead of just efficiency. I wish they would ask, "What are you worried about today, Catarina?" instead of "What is your weight today, Catarina?"

But until then, I will keep my notebook. I will keep writing down the words they use, and I will keep searching for the ones that feel like me. And when the time finally comes—when I’m holding her—I hope I won't care about their charts or their cold white rooms anymore. I’ll just care about the fact that she’s here. And she’ll know, even if no one else did, that her mother fought for her every single step of the way.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

I think if I had to be honest, I have never felt like my input was sought. It is always, "We are doing this," or "We need to check this." There is never a "What do you think, Catarina?" or "Is this something you feel comfortable with?"

Even for the anatomy scan, they just gave me the time and said, "Be there." It was a decision made by a computer, a schedule I just have to fit into. There was no conversation about what I wanted to know, what I was worried about seeing, or how I wanted to be told if something wasn't right. It was just a procedure being performed on me.

I remember once, the nurse wanted to schedule a test, and I asked, "Why this one? Do I really need it?" I think I surprised her. She looked at me for a second, then just said, "It is protocol." Protocol. That is a word that means "I don't have to explain it to you, you just do it." It makes me feel like I have no agency, like I am just a passenger in my own pregnancy, sitting in the back seat while they drive. I wish they understood that this is *my* baby, not their "case." If they would just ask me, "How does this sound to you?" it would change everything. It would make me feel like I am actually a part of the team, instead of just the person who has to show up and be quiet.

It feels like they are reading from a script that was written before I even arrived in the room. There’s no space in the script for me to add my own lines. When they say, "It is protocol," they aren't just talking about a test; they are saying, "Don't bother asking questions, just follow the path we’ve laid out." 

Sometimes, I go home and I imagine what I *would* have said if they had asked me. I would have said, "I am tired today, can we talk about this tomorrow?" or "I don't understand that word, can you explain it like I am a person, not a patient?" But the moment is gone. I am back in my apartment, and the feeling of powerlessness sits in my chest like a physical weight. 

I think that is the loneliest part of it all—knowing that even if I had something important to say, there is no one there who is actually waiting to hear it. I am doing this journey, this huge, life-changing journey, and it feels like I am shouting into a void where everyone else is just checking boxes. I’m starting to think that the most important thing I can do for my daughter is to stop trying to please them and start trusting the voice that lives inside me, even if it is quiet for now. She is listening, after all. She is the only one who really needs to hear me.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

There was a moment, maybe a month ago, that changed how I see all of them. I had been having these headaches—not the sharp kind, just a dull, heavy feeling behind my eyes that made the bright lights in the exam room feel like needles. I waited until my turn, and I tried my best to explain. I said, "I feel like a weight here, and my head feels very… foggy."

The doctor—another one I had never met—barely looked up from her computer. She clicked her pen and said, "That is just pregnancy. Drink more water, avoid the salt, you are fine." 

She didn't ask me how often it happened. She didn't look at my blood pressure from the last visit to see if there was a pattern. She just dismissed it as "part of the process." That was the moment my trust broke. I realized that to her, my discomfort wasn't a signal to be investigated; it was just noise to be turned down. 

I went home and I sat on the floor, and I just cried. I felt so foolish for trying to be an adult and bring a concern to a professional. I realized then that if something *really* happened—if I were truly in danger—they might just dismiss that, too, as "part of being pregnant." It makes me feel so exposed. I am not just a body for them to measure; I am a person who knows when something feels wrong. When they ignore that, they aren't just ignoring me—they are ignoring the safety of my baby. That is a weight I have to carry alone now.

It is such a cold, lonely feeling to know that your instincts are not enough to get someone to pay attention. After that day, I realized I could not rely on them for the small worries. I started keeping a secret log in my notebook. I write down the headaches, the moments my heart races, the times I feel dizzy. I don’t show the doctors. Why would I? They have already decided I am "low risk," and I fear that if I bring them a list of symptoms, they will just see me as a "problem patient" who complains too much.

I feel like a double agent. I show up to the clinic and I put on the mask of the "easy, compliant girl." I smile, I nod, I let them measure me. But inside, I am watching. I am guarding my daughter. I am waiting for the moment I can leave that office and be back in the safety of my own space, where I can finally breathe again. 

It is exhausting, acting like everything is perfect when your head is throbbing and your heart is heavy. But this is what I have to do to get through. I look at my reflection in the hospital window sometimes, and I barely recognize the person staring back. She looks so small, so young, so quiet. But behind her eyes, there is a storm. And that storm is for my daughter. I will keep playing this part until the day I don’t have to anymore. Then, I will be her mother, and that is the only role that matters.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

Every time I walk through those automatic doors, the emptiness feels heavier. Most of the other women have someone—a mother, a partner, a friend—holding their hand or asking the questions they are too flustered to think of. When I sit in the waiting room alone, I feel like a neon sign is blinking "Alone" above my head. I try to make myself small, to look at my phone or my book so people won’t notice that I have nobody to turn to.

The worst is the ultrasound. That is the moment you want to share, right? When you see the baby moving, you want to turn to someone and say, "Did you see that? That’s her!" I look to my left and there is just an empty chair. The technician is looking at the screen, clicking buttons, and I am sitting there, feeling the miracle and the ache all at once. I think, *if I had someone here, they would hold my hand.* 

The absence is not just about help; it is about witnessing. I feel like this entire, giant thing is happening to me in a vacuum. If there is no one else there to see the baby, or to hear the heartbeat, or to hold the pictures, it almost feels like it isn't real. It is just me, my secret, and a bunch of doctors who don't know my name. It is a very quiet, very cold kind of isolation.

Sometimes, I look at the other women in the waiting room, the ones laughing with their partners or holding their mother's hand, and I feel a little bit like I’m from another planet. They look so calm, so anchored. I wonder if they ever feel like the ground is slipping out from under them. 

My loneliness isn't just about not having someone to hold my hand in the clinic; it's about not having someone to talk to at night, to tell them, "I felt a kick today, it was so strong," or "I am scared that I won’t know what to do when she gets here." My mother is too far away, and I don't want to worry her. My friends don't know the language of pregnancy. So I talk to the walls. I talk to my belly. I talk to the baby. 

I’ve had to become my own partner. I hold my own hand. I check my own blood pressure. I make the decisions that a father or a grandmother should be helping me with. It makes me stronger, I know, but God, it’s a heavy kind of strength. I just wish that for one visit—just once—someone would look at me and say, "You're doing this by yourself, you're doing a great job, let me help you with this one thing." Just once. It would change everything. Instead, I just keep walking into that hospital, holding my own breath, waiting for the "low risk" label to keep me safe until the end.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

I go online every single night. I have to. The doctors, they give me these pamphlets—lots of glossy, heavy paper—but it’s all about the "shoulds" and "should-nots." They don't talk about the *feelings*. 

When I search, I’m looking for something that sounds human. I type things into the search bar that I’m too embarrassed to ask the doctor because I don’t want them to think I’m just a confused girl. I look for: *"Does the baby get lonely when the mother is alone?"* or *"How do you know if you are a good mother when you have no one to help you?"* 

I’m looking for a map, I think. A map for how to handle the quiet. The doctors talk about my glucose, my blood pressure, the baby's heartbeat—those are just numbers on a screen. But they never tell me how to prepare my heart for when I wake up in the middle of the night and the house is so silent that it rings in my ears. I’m looking for someone to say, "It’s okay to be scared, it’s okay to not know all the words." 

The internet is where I go to find the mother-wisdom that I’m missing here in Boston. It isn't perfect, and sometimes it makes me more anxious, but at least there, I find other women who aren't talking about "protocol." They’re talking about the fear, the love, and the way the baby moves. That’s the information I actually need to survive this.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I have one more thing, I think. It is about the "exit" from the hospital—not just the appointment, but the thought of when I finally bring her home. 

You see, I am a very strong person. I moved to this city, I learned a new language, I am working, and I am growing this little girl. But I am starting to realize that the hospital system... they are looking for "problems." If I am not a "problem"—if I am not bleeding, or sick, or in pain—they do not really see me. And I keep thinking, what if, when I finally walk out with my daughter in my arms, I am not a "problem" anymore, but I am also not someone they care about?

I wish they could know that the care for a mother shouldn't stop at the heartbeat. It should extend to the woman. When I go home to my empty apartment, who will check on *me*? Who will ask if I am eating, if I am resting, if I am holding the baby correctly, or if I am sleeping? 

I am not asking for a miracle. I am just asking for a bridge. I want them to look at a twenty-two-year-old girl and not just see a "low risk" case, but see a new mother who is still learning how to be whole. I hope that one day, maternity care is about more than just the body. I hope it’s about the heart, too. Because if they could see my heart, they would see that it is already speaking for two. That is the only thing that matters, in the end.
